evidence_quotes.quote: I think there is a lot more to look at in regards to unfair practices and dark patterns:

For games, after it reaches end of life, I don't expect official servers to still be running, I just want to be able to launch the game and play the single player experience, and hopefully be able to play with my friends via self-hosted server without any dependency on original infrastructure.
end of life plan that should be disclosed to consumers at time of purchase that should guarantee that the product remains usable when company decides to shut down their online services.

paragraphs.text: I think there is a lot more to look at in regards to unfair practices and dark patterns: - Companies being able to brick/shut down devices you own like in case of Switch 2. - Companies requiring applications, accounts and a lot of data sharing for basic functionality of the product like many treadmills - Companies implementing invasive DRM technology in games that just stops working after few years making your purchased game unplayable due to DRM servers being down, despite the game not having any online elements. - Companies adding online services to single player games that also stop the game from working when the service is down, despite not being required to finish and experience the game - like extra cosmetic items. Stuff like that should never stop the game from working when servers are shut down in future when company decides its no longer profitable. I think all products should be required to have end of life plan that should be disclosed to consumers at time of purchase that should guarantee that the product remains usable when company decides to shut down their online services. From just allowing the device to be controlled without their servers to releasing software required for main functionality of the device. Note that I don't expect ALL features to work the same, just the core functionality. The treadmill should still allow you to run, the camera should still be able to record etc, while extra functionality provided by the company like measuring some statistics and keeping history could be just missing. Same for games, after it reaches end of life, I don't expect official servers to be still running, I just want to be able to launch the game and play the single player experience, and hopefully be able to play with my friends via self-hosted server without any dependency on original infrastructure. For this most of services provided by company are not needed - you don't need complex auth servers, anti cheats, social features, shops etc when playing on your own. All that is needed is software controlling the core aspects of the game and just allowing playing together via LAN instead of needing any connection to company servers. I would want to make it very clear that as a consumer I don't expect, or even don't want the company to be involved in any way AFTER the end of life plan is executed. The company should NOT be required to provide any further support after making sure that the product is usable. So that there is no running costs for the company after support ends - but consumers can just take matters into own hands instead of having a bricked device and software than can no longer work. At the very least the consumers of such devices/software/games should be allowed to reverse engineer the required software without fear of being sued for trying to use a product the company refuses to support, and such attempts should not be blocked by the company purposefully making it harder but company should be then required to release any necessary tools/keys/updates for consumers to be able to change the software of such unsupported devices and be able to connect to own servers.
